attributes.stem:
A 48-year-old woman presents to the endocrinology clinic with a three-month history of palpitations, heat intolerance, and unintentional weight loss. She has no significant medical history and does not take any regular medications. She works as a school teacher and does not smoke or use alcohol.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 98/min, blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, and temperature 36.9°C. There is a palpable, firm nodule in the right lobe of her thyroid gland. No eye signs are present.

Laboratory investigations reveal:

  • Thyroid stimulating hormone <0.01 mIU/L (0.4–4.0)

  • Free thyroxine 32 pmol/L (10–20)

  • Free triiodothyronine 9.2 pmol/L (3.0–6.5).

A thyroid radionuclide scan shows a single area of increased uptake in the right lobe with suppression of uptake in the remainder of the gland (Image).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Thyroid radionuclide scanning is indicated in the evaluation of thyrotoxicosis when the aetiology is unclear, especially if there is a palpable nodule or no clear clinical features of Graves' disease. The scan uses a radioactive tracer (such as technetium or iodine) to assess the functional activity of thyroid tissue. Areas of increased uptake ('hot' nodules) represent hyperfunctioning tissue, while suppressed or absent uptake ('cold' areas) may indicate non-functioning tissue or thyroiditis. In this case, the scan shows a single hyperfunctioning nodule with suppression of the rest of the gland, which is highly suggestive of a toxic adenoma. This investigation is particularly useful for distinguishing between causes of thyrotoxicosis, such as Graves' disease, toxic multinodular goitre, and toxic adenoma.

Toxic adenoma is the most likely diagnosis in this scenario. The presence of a solitary hyperfunctioning nodule on radionuclide scan, with suppression of the surrounding thyroid tissue, is characteristic. This is supported by the clinical features of thyrotoxicosis and the absence of eye signs or diffuse goitre.

Toxic multinodular goitre typically presents with multiple areas of increased uptake on scan, rather than a single hot nodule. The scan in this case shows only one area of increased uptake, making this diagnosis less likely.

Graves' disease usually presents with diffuse increased uptake throughout the thyroid gland and may be associated with eye signs or pretibial myxoedema. The scan findings and absence of these features make Graves' disease unlikely here.

Subacute thyroiditis is characterised by a tender thyroid gland and reduced or absent uptake on radionuclide scan due to follicular cell damage. This patient has a non-tender nodule and increased uptake, which is not consistent with thyroiditis.

Thyroid carcinoma rarely presents as a hot nodule; most thyroid cancers are cold on radionuclide scan. Additionally, carcinoma is not typically associated with thyrotoxicosis unless there is extensive metastatic disease, which is not suggested by this clinical scenario.

This case is highly relevant for junior doctors, as distinguishing between causes of thyrotoxicosis using clinical features and appropriate investigations such as radionuclide scanning is essential for guiding management and referral decisions.
learningPoints: A solitary hyperfunctioning thyroid nodule with suppressed background uptake is most consistent with a toxic adenoma.
Thyroid radionuclide scanning is a key investigation for differentiating causes of thyrotoxicosis.
Definitive management of toxic adenoma is with radioiodine ablation or surgery, not antithyroid drugs alone.
references: Iqbal A, Rehman A. Thyroid Uptake and Scan. [Updated 2022 Oct 3]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK555978/

BMJ Best Practice [Internet]. London (UK): British Medical Association; [cited 2025 Jul 24]. Overview | Overviews specialty and related medical content; Available from: https://bestpractice.bmj.com/topics/en‑us/34

Giovanella L, Avram A, Clerc J. Molecular Imaging for Thyrotoxicosis and Thyroid Nodules. J Nucl Med. 2021 Jul;62(Suppl 2):20S-25S. doi: 10.2967/jnumed.120.246017. PMID: 34230069. Available from: https://jnm.snmjournals.org/content/62/Supplement_2/20S

Mulita F, Anjum F. Thyroid Adenoma. [Updated 2023 Apr 27]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK562252/
Lee JC, Khafagi FA, Harris AM. Thyroid scans. Aust Fam Physician. 2012 Aug;41(8):584–90. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/afp/2012/august/thyroid‑scans

attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
